name: Ashkari
description:
Three thousand years ago, the Ashkari had a homeworld. Ashkar Prime — a warm, arid planet of red deserts and shallow alkaline seas, orbiting a stable main-sequence star that every astrophysical model said would burn steadily for another two billion years. The models were wrong. The star destabilised in a manner still not fully understood, expanding with catastrophic speed and consuming the inner planets within a decade. The Ashkari had perhaps thirty years of warning — enough time to build generation ships, but not enough to save everyone. Of a population of nine billion, roughly six hundred million escaped. The rest burned. Every Ashkari alive today is descended from refugees, and every Ashkari ship, station, and habitat carries somewhere in its structure a fragment of Ashkar Prime — a piece of stone, a vial of sand, a pressed flower sealed in resin. They will never forget what they lost. They have made forgetting a sin.

Three millennia of exile have transformed the Ashkari from planetary settlers into the galaxy's consummate survivors. They are scavengers, traders, information brokers, and reverse-engineers without equal. An Ashkari salvage crew can strip a derelict vessel to its component atoms, understand every piece of technology aboard, and often improve upon it before the original builders have finished writing the incident report. Their economy exists in the margins of other empires — trade routes that nobody else considers profitable, salvage rights that nobody else bothers to claim, information that nobody else thought to sell. They are not wealthy by the standards of territorial empires, but they are never without resources, and they are never where anyone expects them to be.

Ashkari culture is defined by two things: memory and pragmatism. They remember Ashkar Prime with a devotion that borders on religious observance. The Recitation — a daily ritual in which the names of lost cities are spoken aloud — is performed on every Ashkari vessel, every station, every habitat, without exception. Children learn the geography of a world they will never see before they learn to read. But alongside this fierce devotion to the past runs an equally fierce commitment to survival in the present. The Ashkari are not sentimental about methods. They will trade with anyone, work for anyone, learn from anyone. They have no permanent enemies because they cannot afford them, and no permanent allies because they have learned — in the hardest possible way — that nothing is permanent. Their diplomacy is legendary: warm, generous, and utterly transactional. An Ashkari trade negotiator will share a meal with you, learn your children's names, and then extract terms that leave you wondering what just happened.

The great division in Ashkari society is between the Wanderers and the Settlers. For three thousand years, the Wanderers have argued that the fleet is home — that the Ashkari were forged in exile and that to settle a new homeworld would be to betray the memory of the old one. The Settlers counter that a species without roots is a species without a future, and that Ashkar Prime's memory is best honoured by building something permanent in its name. This argument intensifies with every habitable world the Ashkari discover. Some fleets have begun to splinter, with Settler factions breaking away to establish ground-based colonies while Wanderer purists push deeper into unexplored space. The Ashkari have survived stellar catastrophe, millennia of exile, and the hostility of a dozen empires. Whether they can survive their own success — the discovery that they finally have options — remains to be seen.
